items.q: What is the difference between strength and power?
items.correct: Strength is maximum force; power is force times speed of movement.
items.random_wrong: Strength is for the upper body, power for the lower.
items.why: Students conflate strength and power. A strong person can lift heavy slowly; a powerful athlete generates force QUICKLY (sprinter, shot-putter). Power requires both force and velocity.

items.correct: Increases red blood cell production, improving oxygen-carrying capacity.
items.why: Students assume all PEDs work the same way. EPO is specifically for endurance - more red cells = more oxygen to working muscles. Anabolic steroids do something different (promote protein synthesis).

items.q: According to the inverted-U hypothesis, when is performance best?
items.correct: At an optimal moderate level of arousal.
items.why: Students assume 'more psyched up = better'. The inverted-U shows performance rises with arousal up to an optimum, then DECLINES with excess arousal (the athlete chokes). The optimum varies by skill.
Correct answer: Bending rules without breaking them. “Direct cheating” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
Correct answer: Over-conformity to sport norms. “Breaking rules” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: If 15% of athletes admit gamesmanship and 5% admit doping, total admitting deviance (assume non-overlap, %) is?
items.why: Exact answer: 20. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
